shortDefinition: Withdrawing cash from an ATM using your credit card — one of the most expensive ways to use a card, since interest usually starts immediately with no grace period.
definition: Cash withdrawal, also called a cash advance, is when you use your credit card to withdraw cash at an ATM instead of making a purchase. Unlike regular purchases, most Indian credit cards do not offer an interest-free grace period on cash withdrawals — interest typically starts accruing from the day of withdrawal, and a separate cash advance fee is usually charged on top.
whyItMatters: This is generally considered one of the costliest ways to use a credit card, and it's important for beginners to understand this clearly before treating a credit card like a debit card at an ATM. Because interest starts immediately and a flat fee is added upfront, even withdrawing and repaying cash quickly can cost noticeably more than a regular purchase repaid on time.
realLifeExamples: Someone withdraws ₹10,000 in cash using their credit card. The bank charges a cash advance fee (commonly a percentage of the amount withdrawn, often with a minimum charge), and interest starts building from that same day, even if the bill is paid before the due date.

A person withdraws ₹5,000 for an emergency and repays it within two weeks, before their statement is even generated. They're still charged interest for those two weeks plus the cash advance fee, unlike a regular purchase which would have been interest-free if paid by the due date.
commonMistakes: Assuming cash withdrawals get the same interest-free grace period as regular purchases — they generally do not.
Treating a credit card as a backup debit card for routine cash needs, when it's meant to be used sparingly and only in genuine emergencies.
tips: Avoid credit card cash withdrawals unless it's a genuine emergency with no other option, since the cost adds up quickly even for small amounts.
If cash is needed regularly, consider keeping an emergency fund or using a debit card instead, since credit card cash advances are rarely a cost-effective choice.

faqs.question: Is there a limit on how much cash I can withdraw with my credit card?
faqs.answer: Yes, banks usually set a separate, smaller cash withdrawal limit that's a portion of your overall credit limit, rather than allowing the full credit limit to be withdrawn as cash.

faqs.question: Does a cash withdrawal affect my credit score?
faqs.answer: It can, indirectly. It adds to your outstanding balance and utilization, and if the resulting interest and fees make repayment harder, that raises the risk of a missed payment, which does directly affect your score.
